item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Only a living, breathing human being is able to initiate this process upon entering SCP-057.

The phrase "living, breathing" is non-scientific and redundant. I get that it's an idiom, but this stuff is supposed to be technical documentation. Unless they somehow tested it with humans who were alive but not breathing, or breathing but not alive? If you enter it but hold your breath does it not activate?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, although it is worded oddly. Usually an inhalation (breathing in) would be described as "deep", not an exhalation (breathing out). I would have used "long" or "loud" or "heavy" rather than "deep" but maybe the author had their reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I agree, sounds like words are missing there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[Deep exhalation]

Should this be "[Deep exhaustion]"?

item.title: Re: Lacking a possible purpose.
item.content:encoded: The intention was to create something of mysterious origin. Context was intentionally avoided for the purpose of the reader being able to fill in their own gaps. There could perhaps be more context, but the purpose of it would be to further that mystery. I'll give it some thought.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Special Containment Procedures: Site-57 has been constructed to facilitate SCP-057 as relocation is not feasible.

Should it be "to facilitate containment of SCP-057"?
(Honest question, currently sounds odd to me but who knows, may be the right way)

item.title: Re: Lacking a possible purpose.
item.content:encoded: Ah, but that one is famous for being utterly pointless (albeit beautiful). Pointlessness shouldn't really be "assumed", as it could be just as easily assumed for anything of which we don't know the purpose. "Oh, it probably has no purpose" is too much of a cop-out.

item.title: Re: Lacking a possible purpose.
item.content:encoded: Exactly! It'd benefit the entry greatly if some clues, hinting at possible explanations such as yours, were known. Maybe some markings, controls, sounds, known differences in behaviour…

item.title: Re: Lacking a possible purpose.
item.content:encoded:
Complex mechanisms like these are always created with a goal.

Meet the Weltmaschine, a masterpiece of the "completely pointless device" form of sculpture.

item.title: Re: Lacking a possible purpose.
item.content:encoded: Maybe it's an execution chaimber built by a now-extinct society? Maybe it was for ritual use? Maybe it's broken? Maybe it's a security device that allows only those chosen by it's long-dead builders to pass?

item.title: Lacking a possible purpose.
item.content:encoded:
If someone built this thing - and surely it didn't spring into existence all by itself? - it must've had a purpose. Complex mechanisms like these are always created with a goal. If it was to test people, then there should be at least an illusionary hope for passing said test. If it was to perform some action entirely unrelated to personnel wandering its interior, then what was it? Or, at least, where are hints pointing to it?

And as for the supposedly mind-boggling self-preservation choice between dying now and dying later… It's not new at all, sadly. A starving castaway. A child down a well. A terminally ill patient. Any human, come to think of it. We all either die when we choose to, or we die eventually anyway. How is this box of a hydraulic press making it any different?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it but I'm having trouble thinking about how the things move and squish people.

I figured it out, good SCP!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This thing's on-list nickname makes me laugh so hard. Of all the names you could've given this, you call it The Daily Grind. Wonderful.

Very interesting otherwise, too! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a D-class talking; the redundancy is probably intentional.
edit: didn't notice how old this comment was, sorry

item.title: Re: Circle?
item.content:encoded: Oh I like that car idea! Maybe have it remote controlled so that researchers could drive it so that the D-class could sleep inside if need be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good example of a boring concept being saved by good writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had to look up "parallelepiped". +1 for that and the conceptual horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"sonsabitches trapped me in here and now I'm fucking… trapped!"
I recommend changing the first [trapped] to [put] so it isn't redundant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And yet, there is nothing to imply that those questions don't have mundane answers…It's obvious what it was used for, it's a pretty standard torture/execution device. It was probably constructed by some pointlessly maniacal tyrant in ages past. The only interesting questions are how it was made of unbreakable stone and where the remains go to, but just about everything on this site is mysteriously unbreakable, so who really cares…

I think I'll no-vote this one. It just doesn't stir any strong feelings in me.

item.title: Re: Circle?
item.content:encoded: You are exactly right. My original idea was to have this as the "mouth" of some great beast, but the idea never fleshed itself out. Maybe a rewrite is in order?

item.title: Circle?
item.content:encoded:
Do you actually go in a circle once trapped inside? Or is the cylindrical 3x18 meter chamber just the beginning of this thing with the path leading a long distance to parts unknown?

Also, am I correct in understanding that the monoliths are lined up and move in a way that generates a moving "pocket" of safety similar to the peristalsis of the esophagus? http://phbuom.50webs.com/GIT/GITimages/Peristalsis.jpg

If so, is the reason that exploration with machines fail because, like in a throat, the monoliths on the path in front of you are closed unless a human steps in? What if you sent in a human with a small car or some other vehicle that could travel for a considerable distance?

Hmm, writing this comment actually got me thinking, what if this thing actually is something's "throat", and the reason we never recover remains is because they've been shifted to the "stomach".
Thank you for the support!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for your feedback. I'll see what may fit better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like this one for the sheer simplicity of the thing. Only half a page long (without the logs), to the point and quite frankly terrifying. I really feel sorry for the poor guy that unwittingly stepped into it and became its first known victim. Upvoting.

As a small note, the 'dozens of parallelpiped monoliths' might be changed somewhat. Either give a solid number or some short explaination that due to the nature of the thing it is impossible to examine its interior reliably, namely by having the monoliths blocking the view to others while outside and the entire thing constantly moving while inside. Just nitpicking here, but it might be worth taking a look at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As this stands at 75 upvotes, I've decided to leave it as is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I like about this one isn't so much the thing itself, but all the questions it raise in my mind.
Who constructed that thing? Why is it underground? What was it used for?

That said, it does have some flaws (all already mentioned around here). But overall, I'm happy with this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this, definite +1 (even if this discussion thread has been brain-bashingly annoying.) I think The Foundation finding it at complete random is a little silly but w/e

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just lock the door and ignore it.

Type "safe" into the search bar and tell me what you come up with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eja… I guess then it's just personal perspective on the nature of the Foundation. The thing that really drew me in on the site was it's voyeuristic science. I describe it to people as being like stumbling on the documentation records of what equates to the Borg in human form. A secret group of scientific paranormal researchers dedicated to the motto, while simultaneously finding uses for what objects and creatures may be useful for furthering society quietly and funding the security of the hostile and varying indestructable scps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reminds me very much of a Dr. Who episode where the monoliths are slow-moving aliens. They feed off of hemoglobin and crush humans for their blood. People were sacrificed to them to keep them satisfied and immobile. This is just a giant gizzard that only eats humans. If this was a trap room and there was a way to solve it or endure it for x hours until it lets them into a treasure room, the ship's bridge, or something to give a purpose it might be better. The purpose doesn't have to be obvious or even make sense to humans. As it stands, it is more boring than creepy. Just lock the door and ignore it. However, if there was a sign that said: 'This way to the ultimate device to slay Ultimate Dragons', I would be more interested. (for example.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sure, that can happen in-universe. But does it need to be expressed in the article?

These articles aren't detailed reports on everything the Foundation would do with an SCP. They're stories, written to entertain and intrigue. Would 173 really be improved by cleaning logs? Would 914 benefit from detailed diagrams?

Even in-universe, it's perfectly reasonable for the Foundation to leave this alone. It's inert unless disturbed, and the Foundation doesn't like to take chances. Their directive is to contain objects, not experiment on them. If they don't think it's safe, or don't think it's worth the resources, they'll leave well enough alone. Remember, you can't do much with this without killing someone, and even to the Foundation, lives aren't cheap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though I appreciate your nightmare-fetish perspective of 57, I feel like the Foundation wouldn't leave an SCP alone 'because it is sufficiently scary'. As I was reading I feel like it may be a good direction to make experiments involving a D going in with some sort of moterized tool (like an electric wheelchair) and GPS. Intending to determine exactly where the D goes when 57 is active and whether it really goes in a circle, if so, if there are a pre-determined number of circles. Conversely if it does not go in circles, where it may lead. The question of whether it goes further down with time is also a consideration, or if the D even continues to exist on the same plane of existance as the 57 cylinder in front of the researchers during the experiment (it didn't seem clear to me if the cylinder continued to be a cylinder, and if it was entirely dug out on all sides).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually quite enjoyed this one. Maybe it's sad that it is something as simple as the author not voting on their own piece, but that was my first plus. The actual description lost me for a bit, but the interview log caught me again. What made me decide to vote? Quiet sobbing.

As an aside, I almost changed back into a downvote when you mentioned planning on feeding people to this as punishment. I think the wiki is better for moving away from "assigned to Keter duty". The Foundation can be very cruel, but that cruelty is driven by perceived need (at least in my mind). It is what I dislike about articles like the game show studio. Sending in D-class to find out what's going on? Sure! Bootleg copies of the video being passed around for black humor? Absolutely. Sending in more to get another episode? …. Time to find the rating module.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the simplicity of it. A machine burrowed underground with the singular purpose of grinding down humans. Who built this? What powers it? We don't know. But it indiscriminately grinds us down like a stone blender all the same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. ^_^ I think it's good as it is. The bit about the power source would be a bit superfluous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
unless he was rather claustrophobic, the reaction of the D-class is over-exaggerated. I… might have unrealistic expectations of people, hardened criminals, and the Foundation folks in general, but losing his shit over the fact he's walking through an area in the rocks that vaguely looks like where he started forty minutes into the experiment just seems … silly.

Well, it's not so much that he's been there before. It's that he now realizes there is no exit, and thus, no hope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do not expand on this SCP. That would kill it for me.

It plays to a specific fear. It's narrow. That's fine. It's good, most people like it. It doesn't need to be great. It doesn't need some crappy torture tacked onto the end when it's fine where it is now.

It doesn't need some failed experiment to turn it into a power source. Stick that in a foundation tale or something.

Basically what I'm saying is not to water this down for the few people who don't like it. That energy would be better spent writing a new SCP. You can't always appeal to everyone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually kinda like this one more than I like SCP-198. I might, however, be biased, given that reading SCP-198 makes me feel physically ill in a severely unpleasant way, and this one doesn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, a power source. That's a good idea. To the drawing board!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea of trying to use it as a power source, more specifically, how spectacularly BAD it could fail. Attempting to brace the door or columns do that they don't crush the person, for example, could cause something stupendously horrible to happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aww. I was hoping that would last forever. Oh well. Maybe when it's done for real.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, personally. The Foundation would likely keep it around for the purpose of protecting the population from it and possibly because someone hopes to find a way to use it (something has to move the stones and it might be possible to set up a method to make this thing generate power). Alternately, the fact that they never found the first body might mean this is one of those things where it might be bigger than it looks and/or there may actually be a way out if you can walk long enough that goes somewhere else.

Technically speaking, this would be a labyrinth, not a maze, because there's a single path, but we can't blame a panicked D-class for not realizing this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If that's how you cats play it here,

Lynxes

As an aside,don't worry too much. I mean, this is sitting at positive numbers, which is quite an achievement for a first article. Most people's firsts get deleted pretty soon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Totally so. Your link leads to nothing. ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If that's how you cats play it here, I retract my previous comment. I appreciate anyone taking the time to read our SCP whether they enjoy it or not. To each his own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! That's exactly what we were going for. It very well could be a peaceful experience with enough mindpower.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Let's rephrase: Those people who have downvoted feel that the only things they will support on the wiki are things they really like. They downvote because this does not meet their standards. There are people who downvote because the article lacks a rating module, because of bad comma usage, because the word "because" is used too much for their taste. Short of a personal grudge against the author (which you better have really good evidence of, like the voter saying "I have a personal grudge against you"; they might just consistently not like your articles), people can downvote for any reason they want.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really liking something is different than disliking something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To the contrary. It's the only thing that warrants a downvote.
Unlike , say, not liking the author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really? You honestly think you have a right to tell people how they can or can't vote?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Yes it does.

item.title: Re: First SCP!
item.content:encoded: Hello Dexanote :) Thanks for taking the time to give us some constructive criticism!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That doesn't warrant a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hello everyone. I co-wrote this SCP. I just want to clarify that this was by no means a draft. It was only after we got such constructive feedback that we decided we could re-work the idea to make it even better. This was my first SCP and I realize now that is it important to try to view my ideas from every possible perspective before posting. I am still proud of it as it is and I've only been encouraged to improve upon it further. So grateful for all the compliments!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I will keep your advice in mind. I have some expertise in Foundation lore and protocols and that sort of thing since I've been reading and browsing for so so long but this is my first real project submitted to the site so it's bound to be a little rough around the edges and you guys have helped the idea mature(some of you, anyway).

The SCPs that I will submit in the future will be edited with your objections to this one in mind, so you guys have helped out drastically.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ahem.

That so? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (only teasing ya, boss :P)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
((I don't post incomplete articles. I do my article completely on the site, generally ovder the course of 4-12 hours. sometimes, to make sure i don't accidentally delete, i save the page, but it's not posting the article)

Hlewis: Please don't post an article with the intent to revise it later. If you don't have everything ready, don't go with it. Unless an SCP has test logs like 914, or there is somethign wrong with uit, they shouldn't change much after the first 48 hours.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It wasn't a draft. We agreed that we were done. This was our finished product.

The unfinished segments I was referring to are addendums and such, happening in the future. I just wanted to post the finished article, see how it weathered over time and changed my addendums and other such ideas I had in mind based on that.

It most certainly was not a draft. I'm sorry if it came off that way. We had put a lot of time and effort into it and were proud of our finished product. It wasn't until we received criticism that we agreed it needed be revised to better convey our idea, one that we still hold to be very interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (unless you're bright :P then posting incomplete stuff is ok)
